food_name: Gado Gado (Indonesian Vegetable Salad with Peanut Sauce)
region_of_origin: Jakarta, Indonesia (Betawi origin); widespread across Java and Southeast Asia
food_subgroup: Mixed cooked vegetable salad with peanut sauce, fried tempeh, fried tofu, hard-boiled egg, and prawn crackers; 1 plate ~400g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Gado Gado as a specific dish. No fabricated code assigned.
ontology.foodex2_description: Indonesian vegetable salad with peanut sauce (gado gado)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GCE used as closest parent for mixed Indonesian vegetable-peanut sauce dish; no exact FoodEx2 entry for Gado Gado.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Gado Gado as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Gado Gado weighs approximately 400g, comprising roughly 200g blanched vegetables, 80g fried tempeh and tofu, 50g hard-boiled egg, 50g peanut sauce, and 20g prawn crackers.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for South East Asia (2020) — Gado Gado; cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 for Gado Gado as a composite dish. Cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo database for sodium and energy plausibility. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) estimated from component-level data within the same approved source framework. All values are per 100g of the composite dish as served.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — analogous estimate for mixed vegetable salad with peanut sauce; peer-reviewed GI literature for legume-vegetable composite dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Gado Gado exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~45 is estimated based on the closest analogous composite dish: mixed vegetable salad with legume-based sauce (peanut), which is classified as low GI. The high dietary fibre content from vegetables (cabbage, long beans, bean sprouts), the fat and protein from peanut sauce, tempeh, tofu, and egg all significantly attenuate glucose absorption. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 40g) / 100 = 18.0 for a 400g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 600mg per 400g plate (~30% of WHO 2000mg daily limit), primarily from peanut sauce and shrimp paste (terasi)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension may consume Gado Gado in moderation; request reduced peanut sauce or sauce on the side to limit sodium intake to below 600mg per serving, and avoid adding extra soy sauce or sambal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate glycemic load (~18 per 400g plate), with 8g dietary fibre, 16g protein, and MUFA/PUFA-dominant fat from peanut sauce all attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Gado Gado is one of the most glycaemically favourable Indonesian restaurant meals for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the combination of high-fibre vegetables, plant proteins (tempeh, tofu), egg, and peanut sauce fat produces a blunted postprandial glucose response, making it suitable as a regular meal choice when portion size is controlled to one standard plate (~400g).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fat profile is predominantly MUFA/PUFA from peanut sauce with low saturated fat (1.2g/100g); high dietary fibre (8g per plate) and plant sterols from tempeh and tofu support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Gado Gado is suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its favourable fat profile (MUFA/PUFA-dominant peanut sauce, low saturated fat), high dietary fibre, and plant-based protein sources; sodium should be monitored and prawn crackers limited to reduce overall saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (8g per plate), MUFA/PUFA-dominant fat, diverse polyphenol-rich vegetable array, and complementary plant proteins with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Gado Gado is considered one of the most anti-inflammatory Indonesian restaurant dishes due to its high-fibre vegetable base, peanut sauce MUFA/PUFA fat profile, and three complementary protein sources; it is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as a regular meal choice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gado Gado is a celebrated Indonesian vegetable salad dish originating from the Betawi culture of Jakarta, widely enjoyed across Java, Malaysia, and Singapore. The name literally means 'mix-mix' in Indonesian. The dish consists of a vibrant assortment of blanched or steamed vegetables — typically cabbage, long beans, bean sprouts, and spinach — combined with fried tofu, fermented soybean cake (tempeh), sliced boiled potato, and hard-boiled egg. The defining element is the thick, creamy, slightly spicy peanut sauce (bumbu kacang) poured generously over the ingredients just before serving. It is traditionally garnished with prawn crackers (keropok) or bitter melinjo crackers (emping), providing a delightful textural contrast. Per 400g plate: approximately 448 kcal, 16g protein (from three complementary sources: tempeh, tofu, and egg), 24g fat (MUFA/PUFA-dominant from peanut sauce), 40g carbohydrate, 8g dietary fibre, and approximately 600mg sodium. Glycemic index is approximately 45 (low), glycemic load approximately 18 per plate. Gado Gado is one of the most nutritionally balanced and glycaemically favourable Indonesian restaurant meals available in Singapore, suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension (with sauce moderation), and cardiovascular disease. It is halal and dairy-free. Available at Indonesian restaurants in Singapore including Bumbu Desa and Bali Nusa Indah. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020, cross-checked with HPB Singapore Foodo database. Confidence: high.
common_user_queries: is gado gado good for diabetics
Indonesian vegetable salad with peanut sauce calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 (primary approved regional source) with cross-check against HPB Singapore Foodo database. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous composite dish. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from approved sources. The dish is well-documented in regional food composition literature.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Gado Gado as a composite dish is not separately reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or HPB Singapore Foodo database; MUFA/PUFA are known to be present from peanut sauce but exact per-100g values are not available in approved sources as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation per FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020). GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for mixed vegetable-legume-peanut sauce composite; no direct GI measurement for Gado Gado exists in the database. GL per serving recalculated as (45 × 40g carbohydrate per 400g plate) / 100 = 18.0. Copper (0.1 mg/100g), manganese (0.15 mg/100g), and vitamin K (12.0 mcg/100g) estimated from component-level data within FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 framework; confidence 0.9. Multiple portion sizes exist in the input (350g–450g); 400g is used as the canonical serving size per the dish name and FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 reference. The dish contains prawn crackers (shellfish allergen) and is therefore not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals despite being otherwise halal and dairy-free.
